name: Central Highlands
history: The Central Highlands region, known as Tây Nguyên, has been home to various ethnic minorities for centuries before becoming integrated into the Vietnamese state. During the French colonial period, the area was developed as a plantation zone for coffee and rubber, transforming the landscape and economy. The region saw significant conflict during the Vietnam War, serving as a strategic battleground between North and South Vietnamese forces. In modern times, Tây Nguyên has become one of Vietnam's most important agricultural and economic zones, particularly renowned for coffee production.
culturalNotes: The Central Highlands is distinguished by its rich tapestry of ethnic minorities, including the Ê Đê, Jarai, M'Nông, and Koho peoples, each with unique traditions, languages, and ways of life. Traditional longhouse architecture, colorful textiles, and distinctive musical instruments remain central to local identity, and visitors can experience authentic village life and craftsmanship in communities throughout the region. The area's character is shaped by a blend of indigenous customs, colonial heritage, and modern development, creating a unique cultural landscape quite different from the lowland Vietnamese heartland.
bestTimeToVisit: November to April provides cool, dry weather perfect for coffee plantations and outdoor activities.
languageNotes: While Vietnamese is the official language, many ethnic minority groups speak their own languages and dialects, and older residents may have some French from the colonial era. Learning basic Vietnamese phrases is helpful, though in tourist areas English speakers can be found, and gestures often bridge communication gaps in remote villages.

places.name: Da Lat
places.description: A cool-climate hill station at 1,500m elevation known for its French colonial architecture, pine forests, and romantic atmosphere. The temperate weather, flower gardens, and adventure activities make it the region's premier tourist destination.

activities.detail: Explore colorful seasonal flower displays across multiple themed gardens showcasing local and imported blooms in the cool highland climate.
Walk or cycle around this scenic artificial lake surrounded by pine forests and French colonial villas, popular for sunrise and sunset views.
Tour this surreal, sculptural mansion with eccentric architecture, organic forms, and hidden passages designed by architect Dang Viet Nga.
Ride the scenic ropeway from Thung Lung Valley station to get aerial views of pine forests and distant highlands.
Rappel down waterfalls and navigate canyons in the surrounding jungle with local adventure operators.
Visit historical locations including the Datanla Waterfall area and former bunkers that offer insight into Vietnam's colonial and war history.

food.detail: Locally-raised chicken marinated in lemongrass and local herbs, roasted over charcoal with a distinctive gamey flavor from the cool highland climate.
Creamy dessert made from Dalat's abundant avocados, served as a soft-serve or scooped ice cream unique to the region.
Herbal tea brewed from locally-grown artichokes, believed to have digestive and health benefits, served hot or cold.

food.text: Dalat Strawberry Jam and Fresh Berries
food.detail: The cool climate allows strawberry cultivation; fresh berries and homemade jams are popular souvenirs and breakfast items.
Fresh bamboo shoots harvested from local forests, mixed with herbs, lime, and chili for a crisp, tangy salad served at local markets.

culture.detail: At 1,500m elevation, Da Lat is significantly cooler than lowland Vietnam; bring jackets and sweaters even during summer months.
Many colonial buildings and temples are still active or privately owned; ask permission before photographing and be respectful when visiting religious spaces.
Local markets are most vibrant in early morning; vendors sell fresh flowers, produce, and handicrafts, and it's the best time to experience authentic daily life.
Roads in Da Lat are narrow and winding; be cautious when renting bikes, stay on the left side of the road, and expect frequent fog that reduces visibility.

places.name: Buon Ma Thuot
places.description: The capital of Dak Lak province and the heart of Vietnam's coffee industry, surrounded by vast robusta coffee plantations. It offers insight into coffee production and culture, with plantations open for tours and a growing culinary scene.

activities.text: Trung Nguyen Legend Coffee Estate Tour
activities.detail: Visit one of Vietnam's largest coffee producers to learn about robusta cultivation, processing, and roasting while sampling freshly brewed coffee.
Explore the largest national park in the Central Highlands featuring diverse wildlife, jungle trails, and opportunities to spot elephants and rare bird species.
Take a guided walk through working robusta coffee farms during harvest season to understand traditional hand-picking methods and daily plantation life.
Discover the history and significance of coffee production in Dak Lak province through exhibits, artifacts, and detailed displays about the regional coffee industry.
Learn about the indigenous Ede and Mnong ethnic minority cultures, traditional architecture, and the province's historical development through artifacts and exhibits.
Visit a scenic waterfall located outside the city that offers swimming, picnicking, and views of the surrounding highlands landscape.

food.detail: Sticky rice cooked in bamboo tubes, a traditional highlands specialty often served with grilled fish or meat at local restaurants.
Strong Vietnamese black coffee served over ice, best enjoyed at local coffee shops using freshly roasted beans from nearby plantations.
Grilled meat marinated in local spices and herbs, commonly featuring beef or wild game characteristic of Central Highlands cuisine.
Grilled tapioca crackers often topped with sesame seeds and served as a crispy snack or appetizer at street vendors and restaurants.
Broken rice topped with grilled goat meat, a regional favorite combining the highlands' pastoral tradition with savory local flavors.

culture.text: Coffee is central to daily life
culture.detail: Coffee culture permeates Buon Ma Thuot society—locals consume multiple cups daily and coffee shops serve as important social gathering spaces.
The Ede and Mnong indigenous peoples maintain strong cultural practices; visit villages respectfully and consider hiring local guides who can provide authentic cultural context.
October through December is coffee harvest season when plantations are most active; visiting during this time offers the best opportunity to witness traditional picking and processing.
While haggling is customary at local markets, maintain a friendly demeanor and understand that modest price negotiations are expected rather than aggressive haggling.

places.name: Pleiku
places.description: The capital of Gia Lai province situated on a volcanic plateau with access to ethnic minority villages and pristine natural areas. It serves as a gateway to explore indigenous Ede and Bahnar cultures and their traditional villages.

activities.detail: Explore traditional longhouses of the Ede people in nearby villages like Buon Me Thuot-adjacent settlements, where you can see matrilineal family structures and traditional weaving practices.
Hike through secondary forest to reach this scenic multi-tiered waterfall located about 25 kilometers south of Pleiku, popular during the rainy season.
Tour this French colonial-era building containing artifacts from the American War and insights into the region's military significance as a strategic highland base.
Visit authentic Bahnar villages such as those near Kon Kotu to witness gong-making traditions, traditional architecture with communal meeting houses, and local farming practices.
Trek to this remote waterfall in dense jungle terrain approximately 40 kilometers from Pleiku, requiring local guides for access through minority ethnic lands.
Explore pristine forest reserve with rare wildlife, medicinal plants, and scenic camping spots that require advance permits and local guides for entry.

food.detail: Sticky rice cooked inside bamboo tubes with grilled fish or meat, a signature highland dish served at local markets and ethnic minority villages.
Venison, wild boar, or jungle fowl grilled over charcoal and wrapped in fresh local herbs and edible leaves, commonly found in village homestays.
Square rice cakes filled with local vegetables, bamboo shoots, and highland pork, traditionally eaten during festivals and available year-round at markets.
Local spirit distilled from corn by ethnic minorities, often served ceremonially in communal cups during village gatherings and homestay dinners.
Fresh water snails from mountain streams, grilled with lemongrass and chili, available at evening markets and ethnic restaurants around town.

culture.detail: Gongs hold sacred spiritual significance in Bahnar and Ede communities; ask permission before photographing or touching these instruments as they're used in ceremonies.
Wear long pants and covered shoulders when visiting ethnic minority villages, as this shows respect for local customs and sensibilities around dress.
Learning simple phrases in Ede or Bahnar languages is greatly appreciated; even attempting local greetings opens doors with villagers more than relying on Vietnamese.
When invited to share food in a longhouse or homestay, eating from communal dishes and accepting offered alcohol (even small sips) demonstrates respect for hospitality traditions.

places.name: Kon Tum
places.description: A historic border town on the Se San River known for its French colonial heritage and proximity to ethnic minority communities. The town offers a quieter alternative to other highland cities with access to traditional longhouse villages and stunning river scenery.

activities.detail: A striking wooden cathedral built in 1913 by French missionaries, featuring a distinctive architectural blend of colonial and local influences.
Visit traditional Bahnar ethnic minority longhouses in nearby villages to experience communal living and learn about indigenous highland culture.
Paddle down the scenic Se San River through forested gorges and past limestone cliffs for wildlife spotting and peaceful river scenery.
Browse the bustling central market where ethnic minorities sell local produce, handicrafts, and traditional textiles woven by hand.

activities.text: Yok Don National Park day trip
activities.detail: Venture to this nearby wildlife sanctuary known for elephant encounters, jungle trekking, and rare bird species in primary forest.
Stroll through town to view restored colonial-era buildings including the bishop's residence and old administrative structures.

food.detail: Glutinous rice cooked inside bamboo segments, a signature highland dish often served with grilled fish or meat.
A tangy, aromatic soup made with fresh river fish, tamarind, and local herbs, reflecting Se San River traditions.
Fresh Se San fish grilled whole and seasoned with wild dill and lime, a simple preparation highlighting local catch.
Marinated pork or beef grilled over charcoal, served with sticky rice and foraged greens from highland villages.
Fish wrapped in banana leaves with lemongrass and galangal, a traditional cooking method of the Bahnar people.

culture.detail: Always ask permission before photographing people or entering longhouses, and consider hiring a local guide to ensure cultural sensitivity.
Bronze gongs are sacred in Bahnar culture and central to festivals and daily life; listen respectfully and accept invitations to hear them played.
Kon Tum's architecture and layout reflect its colonial past; understanding this history enriches appreciation of the town's unique blend of Vietnamese and French influences.
Gentle haggling is expected at the market, but approach with friendliness and respect; many vendors are ethnic minorities supporting their families.

places.name: Datanla Falls
places.description: A scenic two-tiered waterfall just 5km from Da Lat featuring a 30-meter drop surrounded by lush forest and hiking trails. Visitors can enjoy the alpine environment and combine the visit with a cable car ride through the canopy.

activities.detail: Trek down stone steps and forest paths to reach the 30-meter main waterfall and cool pools ideal for wading.
Take the cable car through the pine and evergreen forest canopy for aerial views of the surrounding highlands and distant valley.
Follow the trail upstream to discover the upper waterfall tier and smaller cascades hidden in the dense forest.
Stroll through marked trails lined with alpine vegetation, moss-covered rocks, and endemic highland flora unique to Da Lat's cool climate.
Position yourself near the main falls during morning or afternoon light to capture mist-created rainbows over the cascade.
Swim or bathe in the clear, cool pool at the base of the waterfall during warmer afternoon hours.

food.detail: A creamy cold smoothie made with locally-grown avocados and condensed milk, reflecting Da Lat's reputation as Vietnam's fruit basket.
Fresh-caught river or stream fish grilled over charcoal with herbs, often served at small stalls near the falls entrance.

food.text: Da Lat Chicken (Gà Nướng Da Lat)
food.detail: Free-range highland chicken marinated in lemongrass and grilled, a specialty reflecting the region's cooler farming practices.
Simple highland vegetables including corn, taro, and sweet potato boiled and served with salt, sourced from Da Lat's extensive gardens.
Aromatic soup featuring forest mushrooms foraged from the highland region, often found at nearby restaurants and food stalls.

culture.detail: Stay on marked trails, do not litter, and avoid disturbing the delicate alpine ecosystem and the local Kinh and Tày communities who have stewarded these highlands for generations.
Da Lat's highlands are cool year-round (12–20°C), so bring a light jacket even in summer, reflecting local awareness of the region's unique subtropical highland climate.
Arrive at dawn to see mist rising from the forest, fewer crowds, and optimal light for photography—a preference shared by both locals and experienced highland trekkers.
Hiring a local guide from Da Lat enriches your experience with knowledge of forest ecology, Tày customs, and lesser-known trails while supporting the community economy.

places.name: Prenn Waterfall
places.description: A picturesque 40-meter waterfall located on the road between Da Lat and Thap Cham, accessible via scenic walking paths through pine forest. The cool mountain air and misty landscape create an ethereal atmosphere ideal for photography.

activities.detail: Capture the 40-meter cascade against the misty pine forest backdrop, with the best light typically occurring in early morning when mist is heaviest.
Walk the scenic paths through the cool highland pine forest surrounding the waterfall, experiencing the crisp mountain air and dappled sunlight through the canopy.
Wade or swim in the cool emerald pool at the base of the waterfall on warm days, though water depth and flow vary seasonally.
Arrive before dawn to witness the mist-shrouded waterfall illuminated by early light, when the landscape has an ethereal, almost surreal quality.
Spend quiet time on the viewing platforms breathing the cool, crisp mountain air while listening to the waterfall's roar and forest sounds.
Use Prenn as a natural rest point on the Da Lat to Thap Cham route, combining travel with a refreshing nature break.

food.detail: Try the region's famous creamy avocados, often served fresh or blended into smoothies at nearby roadside stalls near the waterfall entrance.
Roasted or boiled corn sold by local vendors along the Da Lat-Thap Cham road, a simple highland staple perfect for the cool mountain setting.
Fresh strawberries grown in the surrounding highlands, available seasonally at fruit stands near the waterfall, known for their sweetness at this elevation.
Local vendors serve fresh spring water and highland herbal teas near the waterfall, refreshing after the walk through the pine forest.
Simple grilled or boiled vegetables sourced from Da Lat's agricultural areas, often prepared at nearby rest stops with local herbs and mountain spring water.

culture.detail: Many local Vietnamese believe the waterfall and surrounding forest are home to nature spirits; avoid loud behavior and be respectful of the sacred natural space.
The waterfall's flow and appearance change dramatically with monsoon seasons (May-September brings more flow), so plan your visit accordingly for the experience you want.
The pine forest paths and rocks around the waterfall base are slippery year-round; sturdy hiking shoes or waterproof sandals are essential for safety.
Local guides and visitors recommend arriving before 8 AM to avoid crowds and experience the mist-covered ethereal atmosphere the site is known for.

places.name: Lak Lake
places.description: A large natural lake in Dak Lak province surrounded by forested hills and home to the indigenous M'nong people who traditionally use trained elephants. Visitors can boat across the pristine waters, trek to nearby villages, and experience elephant rides in their natural habitat.

activities.detail: Experience riding trained elephants through forests and to waterholes, led by M'nong mahouts who have worked with these animals for generations.
Cruise across the calm freshwater lake to spot birdlife, visit floating villages, and enjoy views of forested hills reflected in pristine waters.
Hike to traditional M'nong settlements like Ban Don or Ban Lac to visit longhouses, meet local families, and learn about indigenous customs and daily life.
Join local fishermen in traditional small-boat fishing to experience age-old techniques and watch sunrise over the lake.
Trek to nearby waterfalls such as Dray Nur and Dray Sap, which flow year-round and offer refreshing swimming spots in jungle settings.
Drift downriver on traditional bamboo rafts through forested valleys, often combined with elephant encounters and stops at M'nong villages.

food.detail: Fresh lake fish wrapped in lemongrass and grilled over charcoal, a signature dish highlighting the catch from Lak Lake itself.
A spiced coconut curry made with wild vegetables, bamboo shoots, and local herbs foraged from the surrounding forests.
Glutinous rice served alongside grilled pork or venison, a staple carbohydrate of M'nong cuisine often eaten communally.
A light, aromatic broth featuring tender bamboo shoots harvested from nearby groves, often with mushrooms and forest herbs.
Fresh morning glory from local gardens quickly stir-fried with garlic and chili, a simple vegetable dish served at most lakeside homestays.

culture.detail: The M'nong mahouts have lifelong bonds with their elephants; ask permission before photographing and listen to their instructions carefully for both your safety and animal welfare.
When visiting M'nong longhouses, cover shoulders and knees as a sign of respect, and remove shoes before entering family spaces.
M'nong villages are living communities, not museums; greet elders first and seek permission from household members before entering or photographing inside longhouses.
If invited to eat with a family, accept graciously and eat from shared dishes; refusing food can be considered impolite in M'nong tradition.

places.name: Buon Don
places.description: A small village on the edge of Lak Lake known as the elephant capital of Vietnam, where the M'nong ethnic minority has trained Asian elephants for centuries. It offers authentic cultural experiences including elephant camps, traditional village stays, and interactions with indigenous communities.

activities.detail: Wade into the water on the back of a trained Asian elephant for a swimming experience unique to the M'nong tradition.
Tour working elephant sanctuaries where you can observe how M'nong mahouts train and care for these gentle giants daily.
Sleep in a traditional stilt house with a local M'nong family to experience their daily life, cooking, and storytelling.
Cruise across Vietnam's largest natural freshwater lake to spot wildlife, visit floating villages, and watch sunsets over the highlands.
Browse the weekly market where M'nong locals sell traditional crafts, textiles, and fresh highland produce.
Attend a cultural performance featuring bronze gong music and traditional M'nong dances in the village gathering area.

food.detail: Fresh local fish seasoned with lemongrass, chili, and salt, cooked over charcoal and served with sticky rice and vegetables.
Glutinous rice cooked inside a hollowed bamboo tube with coconut milk, a specialty of highland M'nong communities.
A rich, aromatic curry made with large local fish named for their shape, flavored with turmeric, galangal, and coconut milk.
Venison or wild boar from nearby forests, marinated in M'nong spice blends and slow-roasted over wood fires.
Glutinous rice served with fresh herbs, edible flowers, and fermented fish sauce as a traditional M'nong staple meal.

culture.detail: Mahouts (elephant trainers) have lifelong bonds with their animals; always follow their instructions and avoid sudden movements or loud noises.
A simple 'Hello' in M'nong (the local language) shows respect and will be warmly received by village elders and families.
When entering a traditional stilt house, always remove your footwear at the entrance as a sign of respect for the family's space.
Sharing food and rice wine with hosts at family dinners is a core M'nong social custom; accepting a small portion shows appreciation.

places.name: Lang Bian Mountain
places.description: The highest peak in the Da Lat area at 2,169 meters offering panoramic views of the plateau and surrounding valleys. The sunrise hike attracts visitors seeking dramatic vistas and an escape into the cool highland environment.

activities.detail: Start before dawn from the main trailhead to reach the 2,169m summit for panoramic views of the Da Lat plateau bathed in golden light.
Take the scenic cable car from the base station to bypass the full hike while still enjoying elevated vistas of surrounding valleys and pine forests.
Explore the cool highland forest ecosystem on marked trails lined with towering pine trees and patches of wildflowers during spring months.
Capture dramatic landscape shots from designated viewpoints that frame the Da Lat valleys, nearby peaks, and terraced agricultural zones.
Pack local produce and enjoy a meal in the open grassland areas near the summit where cool mountain breezes and isolation create a peaceful escape.
Position yourself on the eastern ridge in late afternoon to watch the sun descend over the western plateau, painting the landscape in warm tones.

food.detail: Steep fresh artichoke leaves harvested from highland farms into a delicate herbal tea that aids digestion and is unique to the Da Lat region.
Freshwater fish from local streams grilled over charcoal and seasoned with wild herbs collected from the surrounding highlands, served at mountain-view restaurants.
Homemade preserves made from locally-grown strawberries that thrive in the cool highland climate, sold at roadside stalls near the mountain base.
A traditional highland soup featuring fresh bamboo shoots and local vegetables slow-cooked in bone broth, served warm at family-run eateries near the trailhead.

food.text: Roasted Corn with Highland Herb Butter
food.detail: Fresh corn roasted over mountain fires and brushed with butter infused with wild herbs foraged from the Da Lat plateau.

culture.detail: Stay on marked trails, carry out all waste, and greet other hikers respectfully as the mountain is sacred to local communities and requires mindful trekking practices.
The summit can be 10-15°C cooler than Da Lat town with sudden wind shifts, so layers and a windbreaker are essential even on seemingly mild days.
Hiring local guides from Da Lat villages not only provides safety and cultural insights but directly benefits families who depend on mountain tourism.
Vietnamese visitors traditionally hike at dawn for sunrise viewing as a spiritual practice; arriving early allows you to share in this meaningful custom.

places.name: Yok Don National Park
places.description: Vietnam's largest national park featuring diverse wildlife including elephants, gibbon, and hornbills across 115,545 hectares of pristine forest. The park offers trekking, river boating, and wildlife spotting in one of Southeast Asia's most biodiverse areas.

activities.detail: Ride through the park on the back of trained Asian elephants to experience the forest canopy and spot wildlife in their natural habitat.
Take a guided boat journey along the Serepok River to observe crocodiles, water birds, and the park's riverine ecosystem.
Join guided forest treks at dawn or dusk to search for gibbons, hornbills, wild boar, and other endemic species in their morning or evening activity periods.
Stay overnight in the forest with a ranger guide to experience nocturnal wildlife sounds and early morning bird calls in the pristine wilderness.
Visit traditional stilt houses of the local Ede ethnic group to learn about their culture, traditional crafts, and way of life in the highland region.
Explore the park's grasslands and open areas near Ban Don to observe over 400 bird species including hornbills and eagles.

food.detail: Fresh fish caught from the Serepok River, grilled with lemongrass and served with sticky rice, a local specialty reflecting the park's river ecosystem.
A traditional dish made with fresh bamboo shoots harvested from the forest, cooked with local herbs and wild game common to Ede cuisine.
Slow-cooked wild boar with turmeric and ginger, a hearty highland dish often prepared in local villages surrounding the park.
Sticky rice cooked inside fresh bamboo tubes with peanuts and salt, a portable and traditional food of the Ede people.
Local honey harvested from bees in the park's forest canopy, served as tea or with rice cakes as a traditional refreshment.

culture.detail: Maintain quiet and keep a respectful distance from animals, as sudden movements or noise can disturb them and reduce your chances of sightings.
Always use park-authorized guides who understand animal behavior, forest safety, and can share knowledge of Ede and other highland communities.
When visiting Ede settlements, greet elders and ask permission before photographing or entering homes, as these are private spaces.
Plan visits during the dry season from November to April when trails are accessible and wildlife is more active around water sources.

places.name: BA Ho Waterfall
places.description: A triple-tiered waterfall near Pleiku featuring three cascades with pools suitable for swimming, set in a dense jungle environment. The hike to all three tiers passes through pristine forest and offers a refreshing highland water experience.

activities.detail: Wade and swim in the emerald-green pool at the base of the first waterfall cascade, the easiest tier to reach and most popular for cooling off.
Climb the rocky forest trail to the second cascade, which features a deeper pool surrounded by moss-covered boulders and overhanging jungle canopy.
Trek through dense primary forest to the uppermost cascade, a challenging but rewarding climb offering views of the entire waterfall system and surrounding highlands.
Capture the misty cascades, orchids, and wildlife in their natural habitat, with optimal lighting during early morning when mist rises from the pools.
Pack a meal and relax on the rocks near Tier One, enjoying the sound of cascading water and cool spray throughout your day.
Spot endemic species like the grey peacock-pheasant and various sunbirds in the pristine forest ecosystem surrounding the falls.
Navigate carefully between the connected pools using natural stone formations, a thrilling way to explore all three tiers for experienced hikers.

food.detail: Sticky rice cooked inside bamboo segments, a highland specialty often prepared fresh at local guesthouses and eaten with grilled fish or vegetables.

food.text: Thịt Nướng Các Loại (Grilled Highland Meat)
food.detail: Local grilled beef, pork, or venison seasoned with lemongrass and served with fresh herbs and dipping sauces at roadside stalls near Pleiku.
A tangy tamarind-based broth with river fish, pineapple, and vegetables that refreshes after a hot jungle hike.
A chilled sweet drink made with coconut milk, mung bean, and red bean layers, commonly sold at small shops in Pleiku town.
Fresh river fish from local streams encased in salt and grilled over charcoal, a specialty at family-run restaurants near the waterfall entrance.

culture.text: Respect Local Ede and Jarai Communities
culture.detail: The area is inhabited by indigenous highland peoples; ask permission before photographing locals and consider purchasing handicrafts from community artisans to support the local economy.
Wear sturdy waterproof hiking boots and avoid flip-flops or open-toed sandals due to slippery rocks and leeches in the wet season; long sleeves protect against insects and sun.
Visit the waterfall in early morning before tour groups arrive, allowing for a more peaceful experience and better photography light in the jungle.
Carry out all trash, avoid using soap in the pools, and stay on marked trails to protect the pristine forest ecosystem that locals depend on.

places.name: Ea Kao Village
places.description: A traditional Ede ethnic village near Buon Don where visitors can experience authentic highland culture, traditional longhouse architecture, and indigenous customs. The village offers homestays and guided cultural tours led by local families.

activities.detail: Walk through traditional Ede stilt houses with local guides who explain the architectural design, family structure, and daily life within these multi-generational dwellings.
Watch Ede women at work on traditional looms creating colorful textiles and learn about the patterns and techniques passed down through generations.
Take a guided elephant trek through the surrounding forest to nearby Yak Falls, a popular excursion departing from nearby Buon Don village.
Stroll through the family-managed coffee gardens that surround the village and learn about highland coffee cultivation and processing.
Prepare traditional Ede dishes with a local family in their longhouse kitchen using fresh ingredients from the surrounding gardens.
Experience Ede musical traditions by learning to play bronze gongs and hearing stories behind ceremonial and daily life music.
Join villagers on a guided walk through the forest to identify and collect medicinal and edible plants used in Ede cooking and traditional medicine.

food.detail: Sticky rice roasted inside bamboo tubes with pork, mushrooms, and local herbs, a staple ceremonial and everyday dish among the Ede people.
Freshwater fish caught from nearby streams, grilled whole and flavored with wild lemongrass and chili, typically served with sticky rice.
Slow-cooked beef with forest vegetables, galangal, and turmeric served in a clay pot, reflecting the highland's cattle-herding traditions.
Pork smoked over wood fires and served with fresh jungle herbs, wild vegetables, and fermented fish sauce for dipping.
Sample ruou can, a traditional rice alcohol served communally in ceremonies, which visitors may experience during evening gatherings with host families.

culture.detail: Ede custom requires visitors to leave footwear at the entrance as a sign of respect and to keep the family living space clean.
When offered food, drink, or small gifts during visits, it is polite to accept with both hands and show appreciation, as refusal can be seen as disrespectful.
Meals are family and community affairs; joining your host family and neighbors around the fire for dinner is an expected way to build connection and shows respect for their lifestyle.
While cultural tourism is welcomed, always ask individuals or families before photographing them, their homes, or ceremonies, as some sacred practices require consent.

places.name: Dray Nur Waterfall
places.description: The largest single-drop waterfall in the Central Highlands at 100 meters, located in Dak Nong province with dramatic cliff formations. The powerful cascade and surrounding forest landscape provide spectacular views and swimming opportunities.

activities.detail: Wade or swim in the crystal-clear emerald pool at the base of the 100-meter waterfall, which is deepest and safest during the dry season from November to April.
Capture dramatic shots of the full cascade and surrounding limestone cliffs, with the best light occurring in early morning or late afternoon.
Trek through the lush tropical forest surrounding the waterfall on marked trails to discover secondary falls and diverse vegetation.
Pack local snacks and relax at designated viewing areas that offer panoramic vistas of the waterfall and canyon walls.
Examine the dramatic basalt and limestone cliff faces that frame the waterfall, which reveal geological layers of the Central Highlands.
Watch for endemic bird species and wildlife like gibbons, hornbills, and various primates that inhabit the surrounding forest canopy.

food.detail: This traditional Tet holiday cake filled with pork and mung beans is commonly sold by local vendors near the waterfall entrance.
Freshwater fish caught from nearby streams and grilled with local herbs and dipping sauces at roadside restaurants near Dak Nong.
A humble Central Highlands staple of broken rice served with grilled meat, pickled vegetables, and eggs available at local eateries.
Seasonal soup made with tender bamboo shoots, pork, and local herbs that reflects the forest resources of the region.

culture.text: Respect waterfall spirits and safety signs
culture.detail: Local M'Nong people believe the waterfall is inhabited by spirits, so visitors should follow posted safety guidelines and avoid swimming during heavy rains or flood seasons.

culture.text: Dress modestly and wear proper footwear
culture.detail: The rocky terrain and local customs call for sturdy hiking shoes, secured swimwear, and covered clothing when exploring the trails.
Engaging a M'Nong or Ede ethnic guide enhances your experience with knowledge of forest ecology, local legends, and safe navigation of trails and swimming areas.
Plan your visit between November and April when water levels are manageable, trails are safer, and local festivals in Dak Nong may be occurring.

restaurants.name: Măm Măm Restaurant
restaurants.description: Charming family-run spot specializing in local Đà Lạt dishes with fresh mountain vegetables and authentic flavors.
restaurants.mustOrder: Đà Lạt hotpot with local beef and mushrooms

restaurants.name: Café de la Poste
restaurants.description: Historic colonial-era café serving refined fusion cuisine in an elegant setting overlooking the town.
restaurants.mustOrder: Beef tenderloin with Đà Lạt wine reduction

restaurants.name: Thương Café & Restaurant
restaurants.description: Welcoming vegetarian restaurant serving creative plant-based interpretations of classic Vietnamese dishes.
restaurants.mustOrder: Vegetable spring rolls with peanut sauce

restaurants.name: Mimosa Restaurant & Lounge
restaurants.description: Upscale dining venue with contemporary decor offering international cuisine and curated wine selection.

restaurants.name: La Stella Pizza
restaurants.description: Authentic Italian pizzeria with wood-fired oven serving traditional Neapolitan-style pizzas and homemade pasta.

restaurants.name: Hoa Anh Đào Restaurant
restaurants.address: 98 Phan Đình Phùng, Buôn Mê Thuột
restaurants.description: Local favorite showcasing Ê Đê and traditional Highlands cuisine with game meat and wild vegetables.
restaurants.mustOrder: Deer steak with lemongrass and chili

restaurants.name: Cà Phê Buôn Mê Thuột
restaurants.address: 14 Lý Thái Tổ, Buôn Mê Thuột
restaurants.description: Historic coffee house in the heart of Vietnam's coffee region serving expertly prepared local robusta varieties.
restaurants.mustOrder: Vietnamese egg coffee with condensed milk

restaurants.name: Ấm Cúp Restaurant
restaurants.description: Casual dining establishment famous for hotpot with wild game meats and mountain vegetables from local sources.

restaurants.name: Kontum Restaurant
restaurants.description: Restaurant dedicated to Bahnar and Jarai ethnic cuisine featuring unique grilled meats and bamboo-cooked specialties.
restaurants.mustOrder: Bamboo-tube sticky rice with grilled fish

restaurants.name: Dakbla Restaurant
restaurants.description: Authentic ethnic restaurant serving traditional grilled dishes and fermented foods of Central Highlands minorities.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled sticky rice wrapped in banana leaves

restaurants.name: Quán Ăn Lâm Đồng
restaurants.description: Simple, no-frills eatery serving authentic home-cooked Đà Lạt favorites with fresh local produce daily.

restaurants.name: Terracotta Restaurant
restaurants.description: Upscale restaurant housed in a colonial villa offering sophisticated fusion cuisine with Đà Lạt ingredients.
restaurants.mustOrder: Duck confit with cardamom and red wine jus

restaurants.name: Mô Mơ Café
restaurants.description: Modern casual restaurant in Pleiku offering contemporary Vietnamese cuisine with regional specialties.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled beef with wild pepper and herbs

restaurants.name: Ốc Mây Restaurant
restaurants.address: 76 Lê Thánh Tôn, Buôn Mê Thuột
restaurants.description: Upscale seafood restaurant bringing fresh catches and river specialties to Vietnam's highlands with modern preparation.

restaurants.name: Núi Thơm Restaurant
